sentences.text: Yes.
Well, we-- the food, number one, food was rationed in Plaszow, and was very little.
Let me just give you an idea.
The ration for the day was in the morning, we had a bowl of coffee, so-called coffee.
At noontime, we had a bowl of soup, which most of the time was very watery, once in awhile, depends on what was available, what the rations were.
Sometime was a little bit thicker.
But most of the time, was very watery.
And at the evening, we got a loaf of bread, which had to be divided into six portions, into six people.
And once in a while, we got some sugar or margarine, and that's all.
We were constantly hungry.
You cannot imagine the hunger pain when you're always dreaming how to get something into your stomach.
Beside-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beside being hungry, we were constantly exposed to harassment, to selection, to beating, to hanging, to execution.
In this camp, there was no gas chambers.
There were no crematorium.
But all the executions were done by shooting most of the time.
And they were all buried in mass graves.
Well, the working-- whoever worked in the shop, like I did, had a little bit better.

But the people working on the outside, building the roads or building barracks, and also throwing-- carrying stone from one area to another, they were constantly exposed to harassment and even to be executed, because if you-- they were watched by SS people and Ukrainian people.
And they only were looking for-- for you to make some kind of mistake in order to shoot you.
The mistake was when somebody was, for example, was-- had a wheelbarrow and had to go right on the board and the wheel went off the board was already reason for somebody to be executed.
Or somebody fell, slipped, fell and couldn't get up quickly, was already executed.
It was just constant.
Also, during-- we had to be counted twice a day.
We had a roll call.
There was a big square where every barrack had to be lined up and counted, and the reports were given to the SS people.
During this counting, Amon Goeth many times entertained himself.
How?
He had the two dogs, and the dogs were trained to actually tear people apart.
And he used to take out one or two men from the lineup and just order the dogs, [GERMAN], get him.
The dogs would jump all over the people.
They will tear off the clothing.
They will tear off the flesh.
And there were people-- blood was all over.
And the people were almost dead.
Then Amon Goeth would take his revolver and shoot them.
There were people-- we had execution by hanging.
There were quite a few people were hanged.
People were-- when people didn't work very hard, after the working hours they set up tables.
And some of the people were taken out from the lineup and ordered to strip from waist down, lay on the table.
And the SS people used too-- had whips and used to beat them.
And they used to have to count them 25.
They used to get 25 whips.
And if they missed it, they had to count all over again.
The whips would cut the flesh.
The blood was pouring, so they had a couple of pails of water, and they were pouring water onto people, in order to keep them alive.
All kind of things, you cannot describe.
Even Spielberg show you in the movie where Goeth used to stay on his balcony and shoot people right from the balcony.
This was the-- the worst camp I can imagine, because the harassment and the beating.
Every day, there were a few people missing.
When we came to the barracks, we were always looking around, who is missing because he was shot during the day?

Well, while I was working over there in this machine shop, one day a Jewish policeman, kapo, came to the shop and called my name and ordered me to go to my barrack, take any belongings I have, and report on the police station.
I was really concerned.
I was afraid, because that's the way selections were made.
People were round up from various shops and they were taken to a spot and executed.
He didn't want to tell me what's going to happen.
But I had to follow the orders.
I came to the police station with few belongings, what I had.
Over there, there were about 40 men assembled.
After a while, a truck pulls over.
They loaded us on a truck and they sent us away from this camp.
The ride was very short, because only about 10 miles away from this camp was Schindler's factory.
This was my lucky day.
That's the day I was sent to Schindler's factory.
Sure.
Plaszow.
Yes.
Yes.
The barracks were-- were big, wooden barracks.
In the barracks, the only thing you had along the walls-- and you saw them-- you saw those in many movies-- there were bunkbeds, three tiers bunks.
And that's the way we have a little bit straw and maybe a blanket to cover and that's all what it was.
And there were about sometime-- it depends on the size.
It could be 100 or 150 people in the barrack.
Oh, yeah.
There were different barracks for men, different barracks-- we had no contact with women, except at work we could.
The hygiene condition was terrible.
We didn't have anything to change.
There was no-- once in a while, they were taking us to a shower.
And we didn't have any towels.
We didn't have anything to change.
Actually, I can tell you that the lice were eating us alive in this camp.
It was-- conditions were just unbelievable.
Toilet facilities was another barrack, where there were two 2-by-4s spread out, and there was-- people were sitting in open area, sitting and doing whatever they had to do.
And the washing-- washroom was just another barrack with few faucets and just running water.
That's about all.
But it's not always they will let you in over there.
Once in a while they did, but most of the time you didn't even have time to go to wash your face.
Every day, 7 days a week, 12 hours, 2 shifts, 12-hour shift.
And in this camp, even-- after the work, they found some extra work for you.
They wouldn't let you rest.
Even once, there was one barrack caught fire.
So what the Nazis decide, they will build ponds and fill it up with water in order to-- in case something else happened.
And once after work, I was going to this-- to this work to dig those big ponds.
And we were lined up in five, and everybody had a shovel or a pick to go to work, because we didn't have heavy machinery.
Everything was done manually.
And while we were waiting, one of the SS men came over and ordered one of the men standing next to me to give his tool to me, so I had two tools on my shoulder.
He pulled him out from the lineup.
And a few minutes later the man was dead.
He shot him.
So that's the way even selections were made.
I was so close to be selected, because he could do it just the other way around.
And-- and I-- I was just lucky.
Was there any medical care?
Very, very little.

Everybody-- whoever was sick was even afraid to go to the hospital or to get any help, because if you would be over there, every few days, they were coming in to the hospital and taking out people for execution.
They didn't want anybody to be sick.
They didn't need-- they didn't care, and didn't have any use for sick people.
So the only thing-- so everybody, even if I was sick or somebody was injured during the work, hesitated to go to get any help, because was afraid that they're going to keep him over there.
And the next selection will be-- he will be executed.
Were there Jewish doctors?
Jewish doctors, yes.
Well, there was minimum supplies, yes.
There were some supplies.
I don't know, because luckily I never had the need to go over there.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, once we-- as I mentioned to you, I don't know how my name came up.
I was just lucky, because the conditions in Schindler's factory was like day and night.
Actually, Schindler's factory-- let me tell you a little bit about Schindler.
He came to Poland, like many other Nazis, to seek fortune.
And he took over a pots and pans factory.
This was a good item for a black market.
This was a Jewish factory, who he took over.
In order to make more money and increase production, he had enough influence to bribe some Nazis to give him more people.
And actually, he established a subcamp of this concentration camp, Krakau-Plaszow, because he fenced if off.
There were watchtowers.
The SS people were watching us.
However, in the factory, they never had anything to do with us.
We worked 12 hours a day, two shifts.
However, we had more food, better food.
We had better hygiene condition.
You could go out.
You can wash yourself.
You could do something after your working hours.

Just to give you one example what happened, that because this was a subcamp of the concentration camp, Krakau-Plaszow, every once in a while a few Nazis used to come for inspections, so-called inspections.
Actually, I think they came to be entertained by Schindler, because Schindler had always plenty of liquor, booze, and food, and so to be entertained.
However, they were walking around a courtyard.
And one of the men, not knowing that the Nazis are around, left his barrack in order to go to the bathroom.
And walking through, one off the Nazis spotted him and he ordered him to lay down on the ground.
He took his revolver out and was ready to shoot him.
And Schindler was around and he noticed it.
He grabbed the Nazi by the arm and said to him, wait a minute.
I will give you a bottle of whiskey.
Let the man go.
And the deal was made, and the man survived the war.
So here you got one example how Schindler was always ready and willing to help anybody.
We saw Schindler.
I saw him daily.
Now, I came to the Schindler's factory when Schindler decided-- when he took over, it was a pots and pan, enamel pots and pans factory.
But he knew that by making pots and pans, the Nazis will not fight the war, so he established a munition department.

And then when I came to Schindler's factory, this-- I worked in the munitions department, because, as you know, that some of the people came to Schindler's factory way back-- even they were going out from ghetto, already working for Schindler.
So when I came-- and this was 1943-- this was almost one-- one of the last group of people.
He constantly requested-- by bribing, requested authority to get more and more people.
You could-- I saw him daily, almost.
When he walked into the factory, you could already smell that he's around, because the perfume was all over the place.
He was a heavy smoker.
He used to give people cigarettes by lighting up a cigarette, taking a few puffs, and just throwing the cigarette on the ground, because he knew that some people were very anxious.
They were heavy smokers.
They wanted a cigarette, but it was not available.
And that's how he was giving out cigarette.
He used to buy food on the black market in order to subsidize the rations, because the rations were very small.
You know, we were talking about concentration-- the ration in concentration-- in Krakau-Plaszow.
I don't know if you are aware, but we found out later on that Amon Goeth was actually selling some of the food on the black market instead to give the people food, something to eat.
So they were trying to make money this way.
1944, when the Russian army were coming closer to Poland, Schindler was ordered and all the Nazis ordered to liquidate all the concentration camp and labor camp and send the people into Germany.
At that time, Schindler was ordered to liquidate his factory and send all the people back to concentration camp Krakau-Plaszow.
Yes.
Not at all.
Please.
Emalia was actually the name of the factory, because Emalia in Polish is the enamel which was painted-- the pots and pans were painted with enamel.
Yes.
In-- in Krakau-Plaszow, one day the Nazis announced that anybody has any foreign money, jewelry, anything has to give it up, otherwise you will be executed.
When this day came, what the Nazis did, they actual shot few people in order to scare everybody, what's going to happen to them.

Then the Nazis with suitcases were walking from barrack to barrack, and everybody who had any jewelry had to throw into the suitcase-- jewelry or money, whatever valuable, had to throw, watches, cameras, whatever you had.
Anything what some people brought with them into the camp, they had to give it up.
And then what I did, I had to-- I didn't want to risk my life.
I-- everybody was still-- as bad as this was, everybody wanted to live, didn't want to take any chances.
And I didn't want to take any chances, too.
The-- in the factory was a courtyard and Schindler built barracks.
And there were barracks for women and men, separately.
But there were 700-- eventually, there were 700 men and 300 women.
But we all lived in similar barracks, like in Krakau-Plaszow.
Yes.
Yes.
Was even a better help available in-- in Schindler's factory, when something was wrong, you didn't-- you were not afraid to go in and get help.
But there was also a Jewish doctor.
There was nurses-- there were nurses, Jewish doctor, and they help as much as-- as possible.
And as far as I know, nobody really died in Schindler's factory.
They were heated, yeah.
Minimum, but they were heated.
We knew, sure.
We knew about it.
We-- you know, he had few confidants what he really worked with, because you have to remember that he was a Nazi.
He could not go out and buy the food.
He could not go out and sell the pots and pans on the black market.
This was all done between Jewish confidants and other Polish people, how the business was conducted, because nobody would-- would do any business, actually, with Schindler himself.
But he was the beneficiary of all these-- all those transaction.
In addition, he was giving-- a lot of people were bribing-- tried to bribe him, in order to get to his factory, too.
Well, this we know only by-- I didn't-- I can't tell you that I witness anybody.
But we know about it, we've heard about it, that some people which were through-- again, through contact with confidants, were trying to get into the factory and buy theirself in.

Well, through-- again, the Jewish-- Jewish policeman, the kapos, they had contact, and they were giving them the money and then somehow money or jewelry, you know, how Schindler had a lot jewelry, because he needed this also for bribing.
Don't forget that in the factory there were daily parties.
He was entertaining many officials.
And that's how he actually got more and more people, and could do certain things, by bribing some other German officials.
Don't forget this was a war.
And they came also to Poland to make fortune.
Everybody was on the lookout to get some extra money or jewelry or whatever.
And it was going on quite a bit.
I was young at that time.
I didn't do-- I didn't pay anybody off.
And I was just, as I say, I was just lucky, like was many other people, like my wife, who with her sister was just lucky that she was sent to Schindler's factory.
And she will tell you this story.
I was about 21, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
He-- first of all, he needed, because he tried to increase the production.
Then when he established munition department, then this was another area what he needed a lot of people.
He needed skill people.
And maybe that's why I was sent to his factory, because I was already working in Krakau-Plaszow, making handcuffs for the gestapo.
I already knew some machining.
I knew how to operate certain machines.
And when he was tooling up for the making shells for the army, that's-- possibly that's how my name came out, because they tried to get more and more skill people.
And some people learned the trade over there, too.
And some didn't need skill.
For example, in pots and pans, they-- they were less skill people.
In the munition part, they had to be more skill people, because there was more precision work done.
But certain things-- like-- my wife will tell you when she was in Schindler's factory, she was a painter.
She was painting the pots and pans.
You had to know how to do it.
And you had to-- it was very hard work.
Believe me, we worked 12 hours a day, 7 days a week, and it was very hard work.
But for-- from work, we were not afraid.
We tried to do the best job, in order to satisfy Schindler and satisfy his requirement.
Department.
Yes.
This was we were drawing shells for the guns.
Somebody said it was for V-2, for the missiles.
Somebody said for guns.
We don't know exactly, but we-- there was an item which was needed to fight the war.
No.
He was-- many times, he was away.
Most of the time he was in the office.
But just to see him-- to see him walking around made us to feel better, you know, somebody who cares, somebody who is-- and he didn't spoke to us, didn't ever spoke to us.
Just gave you a nice look was enough, too, that he's around, and we felt better.
Along with this, he was also bribing the guards, the SS people around, that they wouldn't harass us after the work, because actually, after the working hours, we belonged to the SS people.
We didn't belong to the-- to Schindler.
But he did everything possible to make conditions a little bearable.
And I-- I could say that he was a remarkable man.
We owed him a lot.

I don't-- there was no guarantee that we were going to survive the war, even with Schindler, because once we were ordered to go back to Plaszow, when we start liquidating his factory, there was no guarantee at all.
We were again under the Nazis' SS rules.
And Amon Goeth was in charge of us.
When the Russian army came, coming closer, and Schindler--
Well, because they-- they didn't want the survivors or prisoners, they didn't want to leave behind.
First of all, they thought they might need them for some other work.
And second thing, they didn't want to show what they were doing.
They didn't want any witnesses, right after the Russian army took over Poland, Krakow.
They didn't want to bring to open what they were doing, how they were killing innocent people.
Well, we felt-- already by rumors, we heard it.
When they said you have to be evacuated, we knew that what was the reason.
A group of people-- I was included in them-- were left behind.
And we had to disassemble the munition part of the factory, put the heavy machinery on a train, and we sent them to Czechoslovakia.
Rest of the-- and after we completed this job, we were also sent back to Krakau-Plaszow.
And actually, we were in Plaszow for a short time.
Just a few days.
They loaded us in the cattle cars, and they sent us away to Gross Rosen concentration camp, Gross Rosen in Germany, the 700 men.
And that's the time where Schindler's List was actually established, when all the people, the 700 men and 300 women, went back to Krakau-Plaszow, to concentration camp.
Schindler worked very hard and tried very hard to get his 1,000 people, 700 men and 300 women, back to Czechoslovakia, when he will reestablish the munition factory.
Under the-- he was selling-- his selling point was that we are already professional.
We are trained.
We know what to do, how to produce the munition.
And that's the reason, he said, I want my 1,000 people to be transferred to Brnenec, Czechoslovakia.
And he succeeded.
It was--
